assertions.assertion: Defines a meta function that returns varying N (e.g., np.random.randint(10, 200))
assertions.evidence: Lines 56-58: def meta_fn(): return dict(N=rng.integers(10, 201)) — returns varying N uniformly from [10, 200].

assertions.assertion: Uses bf.make_simulator() with meta_fn= for the variable sample size
Uses bf.Adapter() with .as_set() on the (x, y) observations
Concatenates x and y into summary_variables as a (N, 2) set
assertions.evidence: Line 115: .concatenate(["x", "y"], into="summary_variables") — after .as_set() reshapes each to (N,1), concatenation yields (N,2).

assertions.assertion: Uses .broadcast() for N to match batch dimensions
assertions.evidence: Line 109: .broadcast("N", to="x") — broadcasts scalar N along batch dim.

assertions.assertion: Routes N (or sqrt(N)) as inference_conditions, not through the summary network
assertions.evidence: Lines 113, 116: .sqrt("N") then .rename("N", "inference_conditions") — N is routed as inference_conditions, not into summary_variables.

assertions.assertion: Uses a SetTransformer as the summary network (data is exchangeable given parameters)
Calls .constrain('sigma', lower=0) for the positive noise parameter
Passes validation_data= to the fit_* call
Posterior samples use original parameter names (alpha, beta, sigma)
assertions.evidence: Lines 212-218: samples["alpha"], samples["beta"], samples["sigma"] accessed by original names.

assertions.assertion: Demonstrates or mentions posterior predictive checks reusing the observation model function
assertions.evidence: Lines 224-246: Section 9 performs posterior predictive checks. Line 233 comment: '# Reuse the observation model — never re-implement'.
